data.title: Markets Weekly Outlook - U.S. PCE, Japanese Inflation And Tariffs In Focus
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Week in Review: Tariff Chatter Continues to Drive Market Moves

Markets had another interesting week as US President Donald Trump pledged more tariffs ahead. A potential peace deal appears to be gaining traction between Russia and Ukraine, but this did little to dent the appeal of Safe havens as Gold continues to hold the high ground.

US Equity markets did take a slight hit toward the backend of the week, largely driven by news from Walmart.

The $780 billion retailer reported strong holiday and January sales, thanks to wealthier customers looking for deals. However, it warned that inflation could hurt shoppers, causing its stock value to drop by 6%. This shows that the economic optimism seen after Donald Trump's election may be starting to fade.

Walmart (WMT) acknowledged "uncertain times," which led to a cautious outlook and a $50 billion drop in its market value. Being a key indicator of U.S. spending habits, Walmart's concerns matter. Even Donald Trump admitted that "inflation is back," and research shows consumer confidence in business and jobs fell sharply in January.

Wall Street is also uneasy. David Kelly, a strategist at JPMorgan, warned about risks to economic growth from new policies, including tariffs and deportations. If Walmart is feeling nervous, it could signal a growing wave of fear in the economy.

This was compounded by weak US data, as the S&P Global data showed that U.S. business activity barely grew in February as worries about import tariffs and major government spending cuts increased.

The S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100 both printed fresh highs before a selloff on Thursday and Friday, reflecting the market's concerns about these developments.

On the commodities front, Gold continued its advance this week but did have a bit of a seesaw run. The one concern that bulls may have is that this week's rally failed to print fresh all-time highs. Is this a sign that bullish pressure may be waning?

Oil prices had an interesting week, with gains every day before a significant selloff on Friday left Brent Crude trading flat for the week. Supply jitters continue to persist, but President Trump's wish of lower energy and oil prices clearly is weighing on the minds of market participants. News filtered through today that the US is putting pressure on Iraq to restart the pipeline to Turkey, which could explain the fall in Oil prices, or at least partially so.

On the FX front, the US Dollar struggled this week, largely due to a massive selloff on Thursday. This was a surprise, given that Wednesday FOMC minutes showed the Fed is concerned about the impact of tariffs on inflation. This has led to the idea that rate cuts may be pushed back later in the year than had been previously anticipated.

The Week Ahead: US CPE Will be Key After Hot US CPI data, German Elections are in Focus as Well

Asia Pacific Markets

The main focus this week in the Asia Pacific region for me will be inflation data from Japan, while we also have some Chinese data to monitor.

The week's key focus is Tokyo's inflation data, expected to hold at 3.3% in February. Rising fresh food prices may drive costs up, but energy subsidies should balance this out. The BoJ will monitor if higher food costs, like rice, are affecting consumers. Economic data suggests a slow recovery, with industrial output improving due to exports and retail sales boosted by better wages and more tourists.

China's schedule for economic data is light in the last week of February. On Tuesday, the People's Bank of China is expected to decide on the medium-term lending facility (MLF) rate. Since the focus has shifted to the 7-day reverse repo rate as the main policy tool, no changes to the MLF rate are expected this month. Any surprise, however, could have a knock on effect on emerging markets.

Europe + UK + US

In developed markets, the US CPI release last week really stoked concerns about higher rates for longer. However, Fed Chair Powell was quick to stress how the Fed prefers the PCE data as their inflation gauge and cautioned against reading too much into the CPI release.

Next week's PCE data includes the Fed's preferred inflation measure, the core personal consumer expenditure (PCE) deflator. While the core CPI rose by a concerning 0.4% month-on-month, other data suggests that the core PCE deflator is likely to show a smaller increase of 0.3%. To reach the 2% yearly inflation target, monthly inflation needs to average 0.17%. Due to these factors, it's unlikely we'll see any rate cuts before the September FOMC meeting.

Europe's most industrialized economy faces a big weekend as Germans head to the polls in what will be a key election for Europe as a whole. The German economy faces a host of challenges, while the question of immigration has also been a key campaign point.

The elections could have an impact on both the Euro and German Bunds as well when the market opens on Sunday night.

Chart of the Week

This week's focus is on the US Dollar Index (DXY) after Thursday's selloff and a break of the key level of support at 107.00.

Moving into next week and with the PCE data on the horizon, will the DXY rebound? That is the big question for market participants.

Currently, the DXY has found support at the 100-day MA, resting at 106.47 with a bullish inside bar candle close on Friday.

This leaves me to believe that we could get some bullish strength on Monday and perhaps a retest of the 107.00 handle.

More tariff chatter could help propel the DXY above the support turned resistance at 107.00, however in the absence of tariff chatter the DXY may grind sideways until the release of the PCE Data.

US Dollar Index Daily Chart - February 21, 2025

Key Levels to Consider:

Support

  • 106.47
  • 106.13
  • 105.63

Resistance

  • 107.00
  • 108.00
  • 108.49

Original Post

data.title: Allspring Asset Allocation Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

Global central banks continued cutting rates in the fourth quarter, with the Federal Reserve (Fed) determined to further support economic growth. The European Central Bank continued to cut at every meeting while the Bank of England was more careful as inflation there remained sticky. The Chinese central bank and the government provided more stimulus that gave short-term support to emerging markets. However, while Japan is keen to normalize its interest rate policy further, it held off with rate hikes in the fourth quarter as the yen started to strengthen.

At around 2.4%, the level of headline inflation in the U.S. is still above target, but the overall trend is pointing downward at a slower pace than in the previous quarters. Near quarter-end, we saw some inflation levels stabilize at slightly higher levels as the U.S. economy seems to have reaccelerated on the hope of tax and rate cuts. With respect to growth, the big news in the fourth quarter was the clean sweep for the Republicans in the U.S. election. Initially, we saw a strong rally in equities and bond yields rose in anticipation of strong fiscal policy support. Outside of the U.S., the growth picture remained bleak with the eurozone and the United Kingdom barely growing and China fighting deflation.

Over the quarter, the Russell 3000 Index was up 2.62% but the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index was down 3.06% as rates on the long end of the curve went up despite rate cuts by the Fed. Renewed strength in the U.S. dollar (USDOLLAR, DXY) served as a headwind for non-U.S. stocks, and the MSCI ACWI ex USA Index (Net) gave back -7.60% for U.S. investors.

Current month-end performance is available at the fund’s website, allspringglobal.com. Institutional Class shares are sold without a front-end sales charge or contingent deferred sales charge.

The fund’s gross expense ratio is 0.87%. The fund’s net expense ratio is 0.75%. The manager has contractually committed, through August 31, 2026, to waive fees and/or reimburse expenses to the extent necessary to cap the fund’s total annual fund operating expenses after fee waivers at 0.75% for the Institutional Class. Brokerage commissions, stamp duty fees, interest, taxes, acquired fund fees and expenses (if any) from funds in which the underlying affiliated master portfolios and funds invest and from money market funds, and extraordinary expenses are excluded from the expense cap. All other acquired fund fees and expenses from the affiliated master portfolios and funds are included in the expense cap. Prior to or after the commitment expiration date, the cap may be increased or the commitment to maintain the cap may be terminated only with the approval of the Board of Trustees. The expense ratio paid by an investor is the net expense ratio (the total annual fund operating expenses after fee waivers) as stated in the prospectus.

Portfolio positioning: Overall Asset Allocation

The portfolio’s neutral allocation target is 35% fixed income, 45% U.S. equity, and 20% foreign equity, and at quarter-end, the effective allocation revealed an overweight to both U.S. equity and fixed income investments. With respect to our tactical asset allocation positioning, we started the quarter with a balanced exposure overweight equities and U.S. Treasury bonds. We continued our bias toward U.S. equities over emerging market equities and preference for information technology stocks over the broader index. Over the quarter, we added equity exposure through an overweight to U.S. equities versus Australian and Chinese equities, and we increased our U.S. dollar long position versus the New Zealand dollar, British pound, and euro currencies, as we saw continued strength in the U.S. economy while international markets continued to struggle growth-wise.

Underlying Funds

Six of 10 of the underlying equity investment strategies employed in the fund underperformed their relative style-specific benchmarks over the period. The relative underperformance was broad based, with stock selection being the largest detractor. The fixed income investments did well on a relative basis, with exposure to the securitized and inflation-sensitive securities contributing to relative performance.

PORTFOLIO ALLOCATIONS (%)

Sources: Bloomberg Finance L.P., MSCI, Russell, and Allspring Global Investments.

The effective allocation reflects the effect of the tactical futures overlay that may be in place. Effective allocations are subject to change and may have changed since the date specified. Percent total may not add to 100% due to futures positions.

Portfolio positioning: Outlook

Monetary policy in the U.S. is continuing to get looser, though the Fed likely will take a more cautious approach given the still-elevated core inflation readings and the prospect of more Trump-induced fiscal stimulus to propel U.S. economic growth higher.

Real gross domestic product growth in the U.S. is likely to rise from 2.5% to around 3%, though medium-term growth is likely to moderate back to trend growth of around 2.5%, as fiscal stimulus is already very loose and the marginal impact might not be as big as under the first Trump administration. The eurozone is dragged down by France and Germany, with Germany probably already in recession and consumer sentiment, as well as manufacturing, very weak. Equity earnings momentum has continued to slow into the fourth quarter, though it is likely to pick back up to 12% later in the year, driven by lower interest rates and continued fiscal stimulus as well as robust U.S. consumer sentiment. International earnings are likely to remain volatile and majorly affected by potential U.S. trade tariffs. On the other hand, more fiscal stimulus from China should support international investor sentiment. Bonds should continue to benefit from further rate cuts, although, shorter term, a lot of optimism is currently priced into the market. The short-term interest rate market is expecting another two cuts in 2025, though if real yields remain elevated, we might see more rate cuts. European central banks are expected to cut much more aggressively this year, and China needs to provide more stimulus to turn consumer sentiment around. Cross-asset wise, we remain overweight equities and bonds versus commodities as inflation continues to slow. Equity wise, we prefer U.S. equities absolute and relative to international markets given the positive growth differential. On the bond side, we prefer international bonds, as weaker growth and inflation may lead to more rate cuts than in the U.S. Inflation-sensitive assets should continue to underperform the market despite Chinese stimulus as the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries Plus is likely to increase production and global commodity demand remains weak.

data.title: Ariel Investments Ariel Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Performance data quoted represents past performance. Past performance does not guarantee future results. All performance assumes the reinvestment of dividends and capital gains, and represents returns of the Investor Class shares. The investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. Performance data current to the most recent month-end for Ariel Fund may be obtained by visiting our website, arielinvestments.com. For the period ended December 31, 2024, the average annual returns of Ariel Fund (Investor Class) for the 1-, 5-, and 10-year periods were +11.80%, +8.56%, and +7.62%, respectively.

Markets worldwide defied expectations in 2024 led by the dominating performance of mega-cap technology stocks known as the “magnificent seven. 1” Even though gains have been concentrated, the results of the U.S. election, strong earnings momentum and a robust labor market drove bullish sentiment. This ebullience extended beyond the equity market: gold delivered its best year since 2010, bitcoin more than doubled and the U.S. dollar neared a 20-year-high. Meanwhile, uncertainty surrounding the new U.S. administration’s policies and the Federal Reserve signaling slower than anticipated easing in the year ahead drove small cap issues sharply lower in December. Although uncertainty and volatility are likely to remain elevated, the patient investor knows “stock prices trade on fundamentals. And when those solid fundamentals shine through, share prices rise. 2” Against this backdrop, Ariel Fund declined -0.66% in the quarter, falling shy of both the Russell 2500 Value and Russell 2500 Indices, which returned -0.26% and +0.62%, respectively. Over the trailing one-year period, Ariel Fund increased +11.80%, outpacing the Russell 2500 Value Index’s +10.98% return and just short of the Russell 2500 Index +12.00% gain.

Several stocks in the portfolio had strong returns in the quarter. Global cruise vacation company, Royal Caribbean Group (RCL), advanced on another quarterly earnings beat and subsequent raise in full-year guidance. Stronger than anticipated consumer demand, healthy onboard spend, robust pricing and solid cost containment boosted results.

Additionally, RCL is benefitting from several new megaships, more island destinations and a growing addressable market, including younger demographics who are “new-to-cruise.” The resiliency of the core cruise consumer, in combination with management’s superior operational expertise and revised earnings outlook, lays the foundation for RCL to sustain its powerful growth trajectory and strengthen its position as a leader in the global cruise industry.

Shares of global leader in for-profit education, Adtalem Global Education (ATGE), also jumped following another robust quarterly earnings beat driven by accelerating new student enrollment growth and a subsequent raise in fiscal year 2025 guidance. Revenue per student at Chamberlain and Walden also came in better than expected on tuition increases. Meanwhile, we remain encouraged by management’s solid execution of remediation initiatives at the medical and veterinary schools, where revenue growth outperformed and total enrollment trends are improving. As the number one grantor of nursing degrees in the U.S. and the largest producer of African American MDs, PhDs and nurses in the country, we remain confident ATGE will benefit from the healthcare worker shortage in the U.S.

Additionally, alternative asset manager, Carlyle Group Inc. (CG), traded higher over the quarter with the U.S. election widely viewed as a positive catalyst across the industry.

Investors expect the incoming administration to emphasize deregulation and exhibit a greater openness to business combinations compared to the prior regime. Hence, management’s positive commentary around the expectation for increased capital market activity, fee-related earnings growth, private equity fund appreciation as well as returning capital to shareholders via buybacks sent shares higher. At current levels, we believe CG trades at a healthy discount to our private market value estimate.

By comparison, shares of manufacturer and distributor of floorcovering products, Mohawk Industries Inc. (MHK) declined in the period as consumer demand and pricing remain under pressure due to secular headwinds in the housing market. Although quarterly earnings results were in-line with expectations, management lowered near-term guidance to account for losses resulting from recent U.S. hurricanes.

Meanwhile, management continues to successfully execute on productivity and cost restructuring initiatives, while also preparing the business for share gains as demand recovers. In our view, MHK’s healthy balance sheet and progress managing through economic cycles should help the company benefit from long-term growth in residential remodeling, new home construction and commercial projects.

U.S. direct-to-consumer pool and spa care services company, Leslie’s Inc. (LESL) also traded lower following another consecutive quarter of disappointing financial and operating results. Soft consumer demand driven by weather-related headwinds and continued price sensitivity on large discretionary purchases weighed on the top-line. Product margins also remained under pressure as the company struggled to unwind its higher-cost inventory. In response, LESL’s new executive leadership team introduced three key strategic initiatives focused on personalizing communication and product offerings by leveraging local consumer information and data across different markets while improving existing-store sales and productivity. Although we have been deeply disappointed with this investment, we are optimistic the new efforts will drive meaningful improvements to the business and position the company for long-term success. At today’s valuation, LESL appears to have more upside than downside and the company’s loyal client base, vertically integrated supply chain, scale advantage and seamless customer experience remain differentiators.

Lastly, Madison Square Garden Entertainment Corp. (MSGE) underperformed in the quarter. Despite the delivery of strong earnings results, shares traded lower following a reduction to fiscal 2025 adjusted operating income guidance. The revision was driven by unique circumstances surrounding concert tour cancellations and higher costs associated with bringing sponsorship sales in-house. Nonetheless, with marquee assets such as New York’s Madison Square Garden, Radio City Music Hall, Beacon Theatre and The Chicago Theater, we believe MSGE is well positioned to capitalize on strong demand for live entertainment. Additionally, new sales and renewal activity in the company’s hospitality business remains robust. MSGE recently announced multi-year sponsorship deals with Lenovo, its subsidiary Motorola Mobility, the Department of Culture and Tourism-Abu Dhabi, as well as a multi-year extension of its sponsorship deal with Verizon. In our view, MSGE’s portfolio generates stable cash flow that should enable further deleveraging. At current levels, the company is trading at a significant discount to our estimate of private market value.

We did not initiate or exit any positions in the quarter.

We believe the U.S. economy looks healthy and expect corporate profits to prove resilient. Although interest rates will likely settle at a higher structural level compared to the past decade and potential policies around tariffs and immigration present uncertainty, we think market friendly policies such as tax cuts and deregulation should support the broadening of the market beyond the Magnificent Seven. Once the performance gap between mega-cap stocks and smaller company counterparts narrows, our portfolios should be rewarded. We strongly believe the disciplined investor that stays the course and consistently owns differentiated businesses with solid competitive positioning and robust balance sheets will deliver superior returns over time.

Footnotes

1 The “Magnificent Seven” are the largest stocks in the S&P 500 Index driving market performance: Apple Inc. (AAPL), Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL), Meta Platforms Inc. (META), Microsoft Corp. (MSFT), NVIDIA Corp. (NVDA) and Tesla, Inc. (TSLA).

2 Hobson, Mellody and John W. Rogers Jr. “What the Stock market Taught Us This Year: Don’t Fall for These Investing Traps.” Wall Street Journal, 5 December 2023.

Investing in small- and mid-cap stocks is riskier and more volatile than investing in large-cap stocks. The intrinsic value of the stocks in which the Fund invests may never be recognized by the broader market. Ariel Fund is often concentrated in fewer sectors than its benchmarks, and its performance may suffer if these sectors underperform the overall stock market. Investing in equity stocks is risky and subject to the volatility of the markets.

Per the Fund’s Prospectus as of February 1, 2024, the Fund’s Investor Class shares had an annual expense ratio of 0.99%.

The opinions expressed are current as of the date of this commentary but are subject to change. The information provided in this commentary does not provide information reasonably sufficient upon which to base an investment decision and should not be considered a recommendation to purchase or sell any particular security. There is no guarantee that any of the views expressed will come to fruition or any investment will perform as described.

As of 12/31/24, Royal Caribbean Cruise, Ltd. constituted 1.8% of Ariel Fund; Adtalem Global Education, Inc. 4.3%; Carlyle Group, Inc. 4.0%; Mohawk Industries, Inc. 3.3%; Leslie’s, Inc. 2.7%; and Madison Square Garden Entertainment Corporation 4.0%. Portfolio holdings are subject to change. The performance of any single portfolio holding is no indication of the performance of other portfolio holdings of Ariel Fund.

A glossary of financial terms provided herein may be found on our website at www.arielinvestments.com.

Index returns reflect the reinvestment of income and other earnings. Indexes are unmanaged, and investors cannot invest directly in an index. The Russell 2500™ Value Index measures the performance of the small to mid-cap value segment of the U.S. equity universe. It includes those Russell 2500 companies with relatively lower price-to-book ratios, lower forecasted growth values and lower sales per share historical growth. Its inception date is July 1, 1995. The Russell 2500™ Index measures the performance of the small to mid-cap segment of the U.S. equity universe, commonly referred to as "smid" cap. The Russell 2500 Index is a subset of the Russell 3000® Index. It includes approximately 2500 of the smallest securities based on a combination of their market cap and current index membership. Its inception date is June 1, 1990. Russell® is a trademark of London Stock Exchange Group, which is the source and owner of the Russell Indexes' trademarks, service marks and copyrights. Neither Russell nor its licensors accept any liability for any errors or omissions in the Russell Indexes or underlying data and no party may rely on any Russell Indexes and/or underlying data contained in this communication. No further distribution of Russell data is permitted without Russell's express written consent. Russell does not promote, sponsor or endorse the content of this communication. The S&P 500® Index is widely regarded as the best gauge of large-cap U.S. equities. It includes 500 leading companies and covers approximately 80% of available U.S. market capitalization. Its inception date is March 4, 1957.

Investors should consider carefully the investment objectives, risks, and charges and expenses before investing. For a current prospectus or summary prospectus which contains this and other information about the funds offered by Ariel Investment Trust, call us at 800-292-7435 or visit our website, Ariel Investments. Please read the prospectus or summary prospectus carefully before investing. Distributed by Ariel Distributors LLC, a wholly-owned subsidiary of Ariel Investments LLC. Ariel Distributors, LLC is a member of the Securities Investor Protection Corporation.

data.title: CEF Weekly Review: Tender Offers Keep Generating Alpha
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our CEF Market Weekly Review, where we discuss closed-end fund ("CEF") market activity from both the bottom-up – highlighting individual fund news and events – as well as the top-down – providing an overview of the broader market. We also try to provide some historical context as well as the relevant themes that look to be driving markets or that investors ought to be mindful of.

This update covers the period through the second week of February. Be sure to check out our other weekly updates covering the business development company ("BDC") as well as the preferreds/baby bond markets for perspectives across the broader income space.

Market Action

Most CEF sectors were up on the week, buoyed by tighter credit spreads and higher stocks. Month-to-date, all sectors are up with EM Equity and Preferreds in the lead.

Discount action remains strong on the fixed-income side in particular.

Market Themes

The Virtus Total Return Fund (ZTR) announced preliminary results of its tender offer where it bought back 10% of outstanding shares at a 2% discount or a price of $6.4386 (stock closed at $5.81 at the end of the week or at a 12% discount).

The pro-ration factor is a very high 31%, resulting in a 6% annualized alpha - the highest in our population of "interval CEFs" or CEFs that offer periodic buybacks. ZTR is a particularly good case given its very generous 10% buy-back amount and a 2% discount.

The fund has a hybrid allocation profile. Its discount is relatively wide in the context of its sector, offering some margin of safety.

Other examples of "interval CEFs" are the Muni fund EIM and a number of BlackRock equity CEFs such as CII, BME and BCX.

Market Commentary

Multi-sector credit CEF Ares Dynamic Credit Allocation Fund (ARDC) cut its distribution by 4.3% to $0.1125 from $0.1175. The most recent shareholder report shows net investment income of $0.1283. Recall that the fund has very favorable interest expense as about half of its financing comes from term preferreds with a weighted average coupon of just 2.8%. That has allowed it to generate pretty efficient net income and partly explains its outperformance in the sector.

However, because its floating-rate assets are less than its floating-rate liabilities, its net income will move lower in the face of lower rates. That should be reflected in the next shareholder report. Our High Income Portfolio held a sizable position in the fund until it re-rated higher. It’s one to keep an eye on in case we see a market downdraft.

Stance And Takeaways

This week we rotated between two of Nuveen's unleveraged tax-exempt CEFs, specifically, from the Municipal Income Fund (NMI) to the Select Maturities Municipal Fund (NIM). The valuation of NIM is much more attractive than that of its counterpart. NIM is a good lower-beta option in the sector due to its lack of leverage and modest duration. Moreover, unleveraged CEFs are generating very efficient investment income due to their discounts and lack of leverage (which is still relatively expensive).

data.title: Allspring Absolute Return Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

Global central banks continued cutting rates in the fourth quarter with the Federal Reserve (Fed) determined to further support economic growth. We did see some divergence on the international side, though, with the European Central Bank continuing to cut at every meeting, while the Bank of England was more careful as inflation remained sticky. The Chinese central bank and government provided more stimulus as well that supported emerging markets in the short term, while Japan held off with rate hikes in the fourth quarter as the yen started to strengthen. Toward the end of the quarter, we saw some stabilization in inflation at slightly higher levels as the U.S. economy seems to have reaccelerated and benefited from the hopes of tax cuts and rate cuts.

Over the quarter, the S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX) was up 2.41% with the tech-heavy Nasdaq up nearly 6%. A strong rebound in technology stocks—particularly Tesla (TSLA) after the U.S. elections—led to U.S. equities outperforming European and Asian equities. Japanese equities performed strongly, benefiting from a more cautious Bank of Japan and a weaker Japanese yen. On the bond side, we could observe the opposite effect after the U.S. elections. A strong rise in real yields close to 2% pushed the nominal U.S. 10-year Treasury (US10Y) yield up by nearly 100 basis points (bps; 100 bps equal 1.00%), from 3.6% to 4.6%. International bonds performed well as the outlook for weaker growth and U.S. trade tariffs affected investor sentiment. The U.S. dollar (USDOLLAR,DXY) started to rise again, benefiting from a slightly more hawkish Fed at the end of the year, while European central banks are expected to still cut significantly in 2025. U.S. growth is likely to continue to outperform other regions, and that also helped the dollar gain. Commodities were slightly down over the quarter, driven by oversupply fears and weaker global demand, while gold was down 1%, though it remained close to its all-time high.

The manager has contractually committed, through August 31, 2025, to waive fees and/or reimburse expenses to the extent necessary to cap the fund’s total annual fund operating expenses after fee waivers at 0.33% for the Institutional Class. Brokerage commissions, stamp duty fees, interest, taxes, acquired fund fees and expenses, and extraordinary expenses are excluded from the expense cap. Prior to or after the commitment expiration date, the cap may be increased or the commitment to maintain the cap may be terminated only with the approval of the Board of Trustees. Without this cap, the fund’s returns would have been lower. The expense ratio paid by an investor is the net expense ratio (the total annual fund operating expenses after fee waivers) as stated in the prospectus.

Portfolio Positioning: Absolute Return Fund Portfolio Allocations (%) at Quarter End

Overall Asset Allocation

Our overall equity exposure was unchanged over the quarter. Our overall duration exposure increased slightly over the quarter, more so internationally than in the U.S. We still shun Japan. We continue to prefer curve risk in rates to outright duration. Within commodities, we continue to run little directional risk with a slight bias to long energy over the quarter and we did add precious metals positions throughout the quarter. Within currencies, the dollar has continued to stand out as an effective trade for us at this point in the cycle.

Performance Drivers

The active components of the portfolio were the key drivers of performance over the quarter, in aggregate adding 0.8% toward portfolio performance. Fixed income directional and relative-value trades, foreign exchanges (FX), and commodities contributed positively. Systematic strategies detracted slightly in the fourth quarter (-0.3%), though exposure to alternatives contributed positively (+0.8%) toward performance. The main detractor from the portfolio was the core beta portfolio (strategic asset allocation), with both equities and bonds having a challenging quarter, detracting 2.8%.

Overlays

Within the systematic strategies,cross-asset trend and commodity mean reversion both detracted 0.15%.

Trend was hampered by the strong reversal in rates over the quarter (-0.9%), while FX contributed strongly, particularly in December (+0.8% over the quarter). The German 10-year bund was the main detractor while the Canadian dollar was the main contributor. Commodity mean reversion detracted 0.17%. Cocoa was the main detractor over the quarter, while exposure to natural gas and sugar added to performance. The Tactical Asset Allocation overlay added 0.7%. Both directional and relative-value trades added to portfolio performance.

Source: Allspring Global Investments.

Portfolio Positioning: Underlying Funds

Equity funds were generally flat against their benchmarks over the quarter. Within fixed income, the Income Plus Fund delivered positive alpha against its benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index, while the Short-Term High Income Fund was flat. Exposure to the Alternative Risk Premia Fund added 0.4% toward portfolio performance over the quarter.

Outlook

Monetary policy in the U.S. has continued to get looser, though the Fed likely will take a more cautious approach given the still-elevated core inflation readings and the prospect of more Trump-induced fiscal stimulus to propel U.S. economic growth higher.

Real gross domestic product growth in the U.S. is likely to rise from 2.5% to around 3%, though medium-term growth is likely to moderate back to trend growth of around 2.5% as fiscal stimulus is already very loose and the marginal impact might not be as big as under the first Trump administration. The eurozone is dragged down by France and Germany, with Germany probably already in recession and consumer sentiment as well as manufacturing very weak. Equity earnings momentum continued to slow in the fourth quarter, though it is likely to pick back up later in 2025, driven by lower interest rates and continued fiscal stimulus as well as a robust U.S. consumer sentiment. International earnings are likely to remain volatile and majorly affected by potential U.S. trade tariffs. On the other hand, more fiscal stimulus from China should support international investor sentiment. Globally, bonds should continue to benefit from further rate cuts, although, short term, a lot of optimism is currently priced; the short-term interest rate market is expecting another two cuts in 2025, though if real yields remain elevated, we might see more rate cuts. European central banks are expected to cut much more aggressively this year, and China likely also needs to provide more stimulus to turn consumer sentiment around. Risks remain to the downside, like higher rates if the labor market begins to accelerate in the

data.title: Allspring Ultra Short-Term Income Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

Resilient services activity backed by strong U.S. consumption continued to drive the U.S. economic expansion forward in the fourth quarter of 2024. The U.S. consumer remained healthy and continued to benefit from elevated income growth, low layoffs, and strong positive wealth effects. The housing and manufacturing sectors remained in mild recessions due to still-elevated financing rates, but sentiment indicators began to trend stronger post the U.S. election results. Labor market worries generally eased despite underlying labor market momentum continuing to gradually slow, with hiring slowing, long-term unemployment increasing, and U-3 unemployment rate ending the quarter just below post-COVID cycle highs. The pace of disinflation slowed, which left year-over-year rates at modestly elevated levels.

The Federal Reserve (Fed) continued its easing program and cut the federal funds rate by a total of 50 basis points (bps; 100 bps equal 1.00%) in the fourth quarter. Developed market central banks were largely parallel with the Fed and continued their easing programs in the fourth quarter with the Central Bank of Brazil and Bank of Japan remaining the key outliers that maintained their bias to hike. Global growth excluding U.S. was generally weak, which was supportive of gradual disinflation, with Chinese economic activity lackluster and stuck in secular deflation.

Figures quoted represent past performance, which is noguarantee of future results, and donotreflect taxes a shareholder maypay on an investment in a fund. Investment return, principal value, and yields of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, maybe worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted and assumes the reinvestment of dividends and capital gains. Current month-end performance is available at the fund’s website, allspringglobal.com. Institutional Class shares are sold without a front-end sales charge or contingent deferred sales charge.

The fund’s gross expense ratio is 0.37%. The fund’s net expense ratio is 0.26%. The manager has contractually committed, through December 31, 2025, to waive fees and/or reimburse expenses to the extent necessary to cap the fund’s total annual fund operating expenses after fee waivers at 0.25% for the Institutional Class. Brokerage commissions, stamp duty fees, interest, taxes, acquired fund fees and expenses (if any), and extraordinary expenses are excluded from the expense cap. Prior to or after the commitment expiration date, the cap maybe increased or the commitment to maintain the cap maybe terminated only with the approval of the Board of Trustees. Without this cap, the fund’s returns would have been lower. The expense ratio paid by an investor is the net expense ratio (the total annual fund operating expenses after fee waivers) as stated in the prospectus.

Portfolio Positioning: Portfolio Attribution* and Strategy

Duration and yield curve—detractor

Duration and curve positioning detracted from performance during the quarter. The portfolio began the period positioned neutral relative to our strategic neutral, which remains long duration relative to the benchmark. Throughout the quarter, as Treasury yields rose, duration was extended. The fund had out-of-benchmark exposures to the 2- and 3-year portion of the yield curve where yields rose during the quarter while rates on the very front end fell.

The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) cut the federal funds rate by 25 bps at both the November and December FOMC meetings. Rates rose across the yield curve throughout the quarter as investors adjusted their expectations for the path of rates in 2025. We expect that credit conditions will remain healthy, though we expect volatility to pick up in 2025. We also see two-way risk to the Fed’s dual mandates of price stability and full employment, complicating the path for rates. The current outlook suggests rates will remain higher for longer. We are carefully watching macro data and will adjust our duration bias based on how the data evolves. Global monetary policy trends will remain a headwind requiring close scrutiny, and as always, we will remain vigilant in our focus on risk given today’s unusual macro and market dynamics.

Quality—contributor Credit-quality allocations contributed to performance. An underweight to AA-rated bonds and overweights to A-rated and BBB-rated debt contributed, as did allocations to high yield sectors. An overweight to AAA-rated debt slightly detracted. Credit spreads traded in low, narrow ranges throughout much of the quarter, with some sectors such as U.S. investment-grade and high yield bonds setting their tightest levels in 20 years during the period. Lower-rated bonds outperformed higher-rated bonds during the quarter. Corporate fundamentals remain healthy, and the election raised expectations that pro-business policies could lower regulatory burdens and support the cycle. Continued strong demand for credit will likely keep spreads rangebound. Overall, we maintain a lower allocation to credit and continue to lean modestly up in quality across the portfolio as even modest spread widening could detract from excess returns for credit.

Sector—contributor

The fund’s overweight to corporate debt and exposures to below-investment-grade securities contributed to performance in the fourth quarter. Also adding to performance were the fund’s underweight to U.S. Treasuries and the out-of-benchmark allocation to securitized sectors. The fund’s allocation to non-agency commercial mortgage-backed securities, asset-backed securities (ABS), and collateralized loan obligations (CLOs) were the largest contributors, as spreads in these subsectors tightened during the period. Non-agency collateralized mortgage obligation (CMO) allocations were neutral to performance.

The fund’s allocation to securitized sectors increased 1% during the quarter as we modestly increased our exposure to ABS. The fund’s largest securitized exposure continues to be ABS, followed by non-agency CMOs and AAA CLOs.

Issue selection—contributor

Issue selection contributed to performance. Within credit, selection in technology, midstream, and automotive contributed. Selection in names like Renesas Electronics Corp. (OTCPK:RNECF), Kroger Co. (KR), Emera Inc. (OTCPK:EMRAF), and South Bow Corp. (SOBO) contributed. Selection in banking, metals and mining, and retailers detracted from performance. Selection in names like Wells Fargo & Co. (WFC), Bank of America (BAC), and Citigroup (C) detracted. We believe rate and spread volatility will persist. We have built optionality into the portfolio and have moved modestly up in quality. We will look to tactically exploit opportunities through timely adjustments to positioning using multiple levers based on our six-month outlook and unbiased approach.

*Performance attribution is discussed gross of expenses. The gross-of-expenses performance attribution does not reflect the deduction of the fund’s expenses as shown in the prospectus.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Market review

Resilient services activity backed by strong U.S. consumption continued to drive the U.S. economic expansion forward in the fourth quarter of 2024. The U.S. consumer remained healthy and continued to benefit from elevated income growth, low layoffs, and strong positive wealth effects. The housing and manufacturing sectors remained in mild recessions due to still-elevated financing rates, but sentiment indicators began to trend stronger post the U.S. election results. Labor market worries generally eased despite underlying labor market momentum continuing to gradually slow, with hiring slowing, long-term unemployment increasing, and U-3 unemployment rate ending the quarter just below post-COVID cycle highs. The pace of disinflation slowed, which left year-over-year rates at modestly elevated levels.

The Federal Reserve (Fed) continued its easing program and cut the federal funds rate by a total of 50 basis points (bps; 100 bps equal 1.00%) in the fourth quarter. Developed market central banks were largely parallel with the Fed and continued their easing programs in the fourth quarter with the Central Bank of Brazil and Bank of Japan remaining the key outliers that maintained their bias to hike. Global growth excluding U.S. was generally weak, which was supportive of gradual disinflation, with Chinese economic activity lackluster and stuck in secular deflation.

The fund’s gross expense ratio is 0.49%. The fund’s net expense ratio is 0.30%. Brokerage commissions, stamp duty fees, interest, taxes, acquired fund fees and expenses (if any), and extraordinary expenses are excluded from the expense cap. Prior to or after the commitment expiration date, the cap maybe increased or the commitment to maintain the cap maybe terminated only with the approval of the Board of Trustees. Without this cap, the fund’s returns would have been lower. The expense ratio paid by an investor is the net expense ratio (the total annual fund operating expenses after fee waivers) as stated in the prospectus.

Portfolio Positioning: Portfolio Attribution* and Strategy

Duration and yield curve—detractor neutral

Duration detracted from performance during the quarter. The portfolio was positioned close to our strategic neutral throughout the period, which is longer than the benchmark. Curve positioning also detracted from performance during the quarter. An overweight to the 6-month segment of the curve contributed, as did an underweight to the 1- and 3-year portions of the curve. This was more than offset, however, by out-of-benchmark allocations beyond 3 years. The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) cut the federal funds rate by 25 bps at both the November and December FOMC meetings. Rates rose across the yield curve throughout the quarter as investors adjusted their expectations for the path of rates in 2025. We expect that credit conditions will remain healthy, though we expect volatility to pick up in 2025. We also see two-way risk to the Fed’s dual mandates of price stability and full employment, complicating the path for rates. The current outlook suggests rates will remain higher for longer. We are carefully watching macro data and will adjust our duration bias based on how the data evolves. Global monetary policy trends will remain a headwind requiring close scrutiny, and as always, we will remain vigilant in our focus on risk given today’s unusual macro and market dynamics.

Quality—contributor

Credit-quality allocations contributed to performance. An underweight to AA-rated debt along with overweights to A-rated and BBB-rated debt added to performance, as did allocations to high yield sectors. An overweight to AAA-rated debt detracted from performance. Credit spreads traded in low, narrow ranges throughout much of the quarter, with some sectors such as U.S. investment-grade and high yield bonds setting their tightest levels in 20 years during the period. Lower-rated bonds outperformed higher-rated bonds during the quarter. Corporate fundamentals remain healthy, and the election raised expectations that pro-business policies could lower regulatory burdens and support the cycle. Continued strong demand for credit will likely keep spreads rangebound. Overall, we maintain a lower allocation to credit and continue to lean modestly up in quality across the portfolio as even modest spread widening could detract from excess returns for credit.

Sector—contributor

Sector allocation contributed to performance. Overweights to corporate credit and securitized sectors and an underweight to U.S. Treasuries contributed to performance. Within securitized, the fund’s out-of-benchmark allocations to asset-backed securities (ABS), commercial mortgage-backed securities, and collateralized loan obligations were the largest contributors. Allocations to non-agency residential mortgage-backed securities and agency mortgage-backed securities ('MBS') contributed to a lesser degree. Out-of-benchmark plus sector exposures to U.S. high yield bonds and loans contributed. European investment-grade and high yield allocations, emerging market debt, and global government bonds detracted from performance.

We remain overweight U.S. securitized exposures, within our neutral range for U.S. investment-grade corporates, and modestly underweight U.S. Treasuries. Within U.S. securitized, we are overweight agency MBS due to limited supply and attractive valuations while also finding opportunities in several ABS subsectors. Throughout the quarter, we modestly shifted our plus sector exposures. We slightly reduced our exposure to liquid global government bonds as the relative yield to U.S. Treasuries worsened. We added small additional exposures to U.S. and European high yield, and we increased exposure to European investment-grade credit given its relative-value advantage over its U.S. dollar (USDOLLAR,DXY) counterparts. Lastly, we have continued our neutral emerging market positioning, with South African local government bonds being our highest-conviction position.

Issue selection—contributor

Issue selection had a positive impact on performance. Within credit, selection in banking, finance companies, metals and mining, and midstream contributed. Selection in names like UBS Group (UBS); The Kroger Co. (KR); Emera Inc. (OTCPK:EMRAF); and Advance Auto Parts, Inc. (AAP) contributed. Selection in retailers, health insurance, and electric power detracted from performance. Selection in names like Citigroup Inc. (C) and the Asian Development Bank detracted. We believe rate volatility will persist and that credit spreads at current levels do not fully compensate investors. We have built optionality into the portfolio and have reduced lower-quality exposures with a focus on high-quality income. We will look to tactically exploit opportunities through timely adjustments to positioning using multiple levers based on our six-month outlook and unbiased approach.

*Performance attribution is discussed gross of expenses. The gross-of-expenses performance attribution does not reflect the deduction of the fund’s expenses as shown in the prospectus.

data.title: Allspring Short Duration Government Bond Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly review

The Bloomberg U.S. 1–3 Year Government Bond Index fell 0.09% for the fourth quarter of 2024. Front-end U.S. Treasury yields were higher as the market scaled back rate-cut expectations through 2025 and 2026. The 2s/5s curve turned positive in the fourth quarter, steepening by 23 basis points (bps; 100 bps equal 1.00%) and ended the year at 13 bps.

The 2-year U.S. Treasury yields (US2Y) rose by 60 bps to end the fourth quarter at 4.24%, up from 3.64% where it ended in the third quarter and nearly flat to where it began the year. The 2s/10s curve continued to steepen in the fourth quarter and ended at 33 bps, steepening 19 bps throughout the quarter.

Market review

The U.S. economy continues to grow around its longer-term trend rate entering 2025, though there are signs that momentum is slowing. Manufacturing surveys remain at low levels and services surveys have been resilient, yet they have been mainly attributed to seasonal demand. Producers and services providers are reporting soft-order backlogs as buyers align to lower inventories. Inflation has improved and should continue to come down, though the process has been slower and more volatile than previously thought. Prices in core services categories accelerated in September and October before cooling somewhat in November. Labor costs remain a concern, having accelerated in recent reports, though they could improve given slowing job growth. Policymakers have embarked on a rate-cutting cycle, noting progress on inflation and a slowing jobs market. The Federal Reserve’s (Fed’s) decision in December to cut interest rates by 25 bps a range from 4.25% to 4.50% highlighting that policy is on a path toward normalization. Nonetheless, the Fed is likely to proceed more cautiously with growth and inflation still trending at or above recent projections. Markets responded positively to the U.S. election, which provided near-term certainty. Treasury Secretary nominee Scott Bessent is proposing a “3-3-3” economic plan: increasing potential economic growth to 3% by encouraging private sector investment, lowering the U.S. fiscal deficit to 3% of gross domestic product by 2028 by restraining government spending, and increasing domestic oil production by 3 million barrels per day. Tariffs could be an additional tool to rebalance trade and achieve foreign policy goals.

Without this cap, the fund’s returns would have been lower. The expense ratio paid by an investor is the net expense ratio (the total annual fund operating expenses after fee waivers) as stated in the prospectus.

Portfolio Positioning: Portfolio Attribution* and Strategy

Security selection—agency mortgages

The exposure to agency mortgages was a slight net detractor this quarter. Positioning in hybrid adjustable-rate mortgages (ARMs) was a contributor this quarter as the seasoned discount securities we own managed to grind tighter. Hybrid ARMs remain the largest contributor to performance by sector this year. Positioning in collateralized mortgage obligations (CMOs) and passthroughs detracted modestly as the increase in volatility this quarter raised convexity hedging costs for both sectors.

Security selection—securitized

Asset-backed securities (ABS) and commercial mortgage-backed securities (''CMBS) were roughly equal contributors to performance in the aggregate during the fourth quarter. Credit card and auto dealer floorplan ABS and single-asset/single-borrower CMBS were the largest contributors. Non-agency residential mortgage-backed securities and private credit student loans detracted during the quarter.

*Performance attribution is discussed gross of expenses. The gross-of-expenses performance attribution does not reflect the deduction of the fund’s expenses as shown in the prospectus.

The fund’s overweight to agency mortgage-backed securities ('MBS') was the biggest detractor within securitized sectors as the MBS index delivered negative excess returns during the quarter. Also detracting from performance was exposure to agency collateralized mortgage obligations, where spreads widened during the quarter. The fund’s overweight to commercial mortgage-backed securities ('CMBS'), primarily agency CMBS, was the largest contributor within securitized sectors as spreads tightened during the period. The fund’s overweight to agency securities in lieu of U.S. Treasuries contributed to performance.

The fund’s overall securitized allocation declined 2% over the period, primarily driven by a reduction in our overweight to MBS.

Issue selection—neutral

Issue selection had a neutral impact on performance. Positioning within MBS contributed to performance, offsetting some of the underperformance at the sector level. Within MBS, we remain overweight production coupons (4.5% to 6.5%) as well as higher-coupon GNMAs.

*Performance attribution is discussed gross of expenses. The gross-of-expenses performance attribution does not reflect the deduction of the fund’s expenses as shown in the prospectus.
